architecture.summary: Dynamic Beam Allocation (DBA) is a modification to standard beam search for neural machine translation that enforces lexical constraints (pre-specified words and phrases must appear in output). It groups hypotheses into banks by the number of constraints they have met, then dynamically allocates slots from a fixed-size beam across these banks at each decoding timestep. Candidates are generated from top-k items, unmet constraints for each hypothesis, and single-best tokens; the beam is filled via an allocation strategy with bank adjustment to redistribute unused slots.

biblio.title: Fast Lexically Constrained Decoding with Dynamic Beam Allocation for Neural Machine Translation

claims.statement: DBA achieves O(1) complexity in the number of constraints, whereas prior work (Grid Beam Search and Constrained Beam Search) achieves linear or exponential complexity
On a Volta P100 GPU, DBA with beam size 10 decodes at approximately 0.6 seconds per sentence regardless of constraint count
DBA with beam size 10 achieves BLEU 26.7 on rand3 constraints, compared to GBS/1 baseline of BLEU 25.6, at equal runtime with fixed rather than variable beam size
Decoding time without pruning is nearly twice that with pruning threshold 20

DBA correctly places constrained words and phrases in output without source-side alignment information, as validated by correlation analysis showing constraint position in reference vs. output (Pearson's r ≈ 0.88)
DBA with very low beam size (e.g., beam 5) relative to constraint count fails to beat unconstrained baseline on four random constraints before BPE, achieving BLEU 23.7 vs. 24.4 unconstrained

The NMT model exhibits reference aversion: forcing the decoder to include even a single word from the reference imposes a cost in model score that is inversely correlated with BLEU score, and this cost grows with the number of constraints added

Without pruning, the decoder generates one finished hypothesis but continues until maximum length, populating the rest of the beam with low-cost garbage in language-model mode, unrelated to source sentences
